description: Improve CLI output clarity: distinguish browser tools from plugin tools in status, reduce noise on subsequent server starts, and improve plugin search for private packages.

userStories.title: Distinguish browser tools from plugin tools in status output
userStories.description: As a user running opentabs status, I want to understand where the tool count comes from so that I am not confused when I see '37 tools' with '0 plugins'.
acceptanceCriteria: opentabs status shows browser tools and plugin tools as separate counts, e.g. 'Browser tools  37' and 'Plugin tools  0' (or 'Tools  37 (37 browser, 0 plugin)')
When plugins are installed, the plugin tool count reflects only plugin-contributed tools
The --json output from opentabs status --json includes both browserToolCount and pluginToolCount fields in the health response
userStories.notes:
In platform/cli/src/commands/status.ts line 103, toolCount is displayed as a single number. The /health endpoint (platform/mcp-server/src/http-routes.ts line 277) computes toolCount as state.registry.toolLookup.size + state.cachedBrowserTools.length. The fix should:

1. In http-routes.ts, add separate browserToolCount and pluginToolCount fields to the health response (in addition to or replacing the combined toolCount for backwards compat).
2. In status.ts, display both counts. A clean format would be: 'Tools  37 (37 browser, 0 plugin)' or separate lines.
3. Keep toolCount in the JSON response for backwards compatibility but add the new fields.

userStories.title: Suppress verbose MCP client config on subsequent server starts
userStories.description: As a user who has already configured my MCP client, I want opentabs start to show a clean minimal output on subsequent runs so that the terminal is not flooded with setup instructions I no longer need.
acceptanceCriteria: On first run (isFirstTime=true), the full MCP client config block with all 4 clients is printed (current behavior preserved)
On subsequent runs (isFirstTime=false), the server prints a compact 2-3 line output: endpoint URL, port, and a note like 'Run opentabs config show --show-secret to see MCP client config'
A new --show-config flag on opentabs start forces printing the full config block even on subsequent runs
userStories.notes:
In platform/cli/src/commands/start.ts, the handleStart function already knows isFirstTime (line 234: const isFirstTime = await autoInitialize(configDir)). The large config block is printed by printMcpClientConfig() starting around line 150. On subsequent runs, instead of printing the full block, print a minimal message like:

  MCP endpoint:  http://localhost:9515/mcp
  Health check:  http://localhost:9515/health
  Log file:      ~/.opentabs/server.log
  Run `opentabs config show --show-secret` to see MCP client configuration.

Add a --show-config option to the start command (commander option) that forces the full block. Check the commander setup near line 225 where the command is defined.

userStories.title: Improve plugin search to show private @opentabs-dev plugins
userStories.description: As a user searching for plugins, I want opentabs plugin search to find and list private @opentabs-dev plugins so that I can discover available official plugins.
acceptanceCriteria: opentabs plugin search (no query) lists all discoverable @opentabs-dev plugins that the user has npm access to
opentabs plugin search slack finds the @opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-slack package
opentabs plugin search with a non-existent query still shows 'No plugins found' appropriately
The search still works for public community plugins via keyword search
userStories.notes:
Root cause: In platform/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts, npmSearchPlugins() (lines 319-339) uses 'npm search keywords:opentabs-plugin --json' which does NOT return private packages from npm. And buildDirectLookupCandidates() returns [] when query is empty (line 300).

Fix approach: When no query is given (or for any search), also probe a known list of official plugin name patterns. Add a function that runs 'npm view @opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-{name} --json' for a set of well-known official plugin names. This is similar to how plugin install already probes @opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-<name> as a candidate.

Alternative better approach: Run 'npm search @opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin --json' (search by scope prefix) to discover private packages the user has access to. Or use 'npm exec -- npm-registry-fetch /-/org/opentabs-dev/package' to list org packages.

Simplest approach: For the no-query case, run a batch of 'npm view @opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-<name> --json 2>/dev/null' in parallel for a known set of official plugin slugs. Maintain this list in a const array. While not auto-discovering, it provides a working search for the known ecosystem. For the query case, already add the @opentabs-dev scoped candidate (which buildDirectLookupCandidates already does for non-empty queries).
